threats.title: Relayer Gas Griefing Fee Theft via Insufficient Gas in processMessage
threats.hypothesis_description: A malicious relayer can call processMessage with deliberately insufficient gas. The EVM's 63/64 rule causes excessivelySafeCall to forward less gas than _message.gasLimit to the target, causing the invocation to fail. The message is marked RETRIABLE and the relayer pockets the entire fee, while the user must pay gas again to retry with no fee incentive for retriers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code does not validate that gasleft() is sufficient to forward _message.gasLimit before calling _invokeMessageCall. On L2 (invocationDelay=0), proof and execution happen atomically in one transaction, giving the relayer full control. This is exploitable by any address without special privileges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasLimit = msg.sender == _message.destOwner ? gasleft() : _message.gasLimit; No check that gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit before forwarding to excessivelySafeCall

preconditions: Message with non-zero fee exists and is proven on destination chain
Relayer is not the destOwner (uses _message.gasLimit path)
On L2 chain (invocationDelay=0) for single-tx atomic exploitation
Message gasLimit is significantly higher than tx overhead gas cost

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer provides tx gas = overhead + small buffer; EVM 63/64 rule gives call min(gasLimit, 63/64 * gasleft) which is far below _message.gasLimit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Relayer profit = message.fee - tx_gas_cost. For messages with reasonable fees (>0.01 ETH) and low gas overhead (~200k gas), profit margin is significant

economic_questions: What is the typical fee-to-gas-cost ratio for bridge messages?
How many RETRIABLE messages exist that were never retried?
Is there a minimum fee enforced at the application layer?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: processMessage with gas = overhead + 50k, verify message becomes RETRIABLE
Measure exact gas overhead for processMessage excluding the external call
Calculate break-even fee amount for griefing attack

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.01 ETH gas cost for tx submission
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: message.fee minus gas cost per message
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on fee; potentially 50-90% of fee value

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time messages with fees are pending on L2
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact, pure fee extraction

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who send bridge messages with non-zero fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of message fee + cost to retry
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate of all pending message fees

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: gasLimit values in typical bridge messages, fee amounts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify 63/64 rule causes silent failure for external call

threats.title: changeBridgedToken Deletes Wrong Mapping Key Leaving Stale Canonical Association
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken, the code executes `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` which is always a no-op (the new token was verified to have no existing mapping). The intended operation is `delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]`. This leaves a stale canonical mapping for the old blacklisted token, breaking the bijection between bridged and canonical tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code bug: the wrong variable is used in the delete statement. While the btokenBlacklist check currently prevents the stale mapping from being directly exploited via sendToken, the inconsistent state could be leveraged in future upgrades or through paths that read bridgedToCanonical without checking the blacklist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] is no-op; should be delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]

preconditions: Owner calls changeBridgedToken to migrate from old to new bridged token
Old bridged token (btokenOld_) has an active bridgedToCanonical mapping

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] where _btokenNew was just verified to have .addr == address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After migration, bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] still maps to canonical token. If blacklist is bypassed or checked inconsistently in upgrade, attacker could use old token as valid bridged token
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Stale mapping allows old token to be treated as valid bridged token in any code path that doesn't check btokenBlacklist

economic_questions: Can the stale mapping be exploited through any existing code path?
Does any function read bridgedToCanonical without checking btokenBlacklist?
Could a future UUPS upgrade introduce a path that uses the stale mapping?
validation_requirements: Verify _handleMessage checks btokenBlacklist before reading bridgedToCanonical
Map all code paths that reference bridgedToCanonical to confirm blacklist coverage
Test changeBridgedToken and verify btokenOld_ mapping persists

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on whether stale mapping can be exploited; potentially full canonical token drain

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol state integrity; indirectly all bridged token holders if exploitable
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potentially total loss of canonical token backing
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Full canonical token balance held by vault

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify no path reads bridgedToCanonical without blacklist check

threats.title: Bridge staticcall Prevents SignalService Proof Caching, Causing Valid Proofs to Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: Bridge._proveSignalReceived uses staticcall to invoke SignalService.proveSignalReceived. However, proveSignalReceived may call _syncChainData (which performs SSTORE) when CacheOption is non-zero. SSTORE in a staticcall context causes the entire staticcall to revert, making the proof appear invalid. This means any proof submitted with cache options through Bridge will be silently rejected.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code has a fundamental mismatch: SignalService.proveSignalReceived is designed to optionally cache chain data (state modifications), but Bridge calls it via staticcall (which prohibits state modifications). Proofs with CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING will always fail when verified through Bridge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _signalService.staticcall(data) where data encodes proveSignalReceived which may SSTORE via _cacheChainData

preconditions: Relayer constructs HopProof with CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING
The proof path includes a hop with cacheStateRoot or cacheSignalRoot conditions met
Bridge calls _proveSignalReceived which uses staticcall

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: staticcall + SSTORE = revert; all non-CACHE_NOTHING proofs fail through Bridge
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayers unaware of this limitation submit proofs with caching, which fail silently. Messages appear unprocessable.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS vector: attacker could confuse relayers or block specific message processing if relayers use caching by default

economic_questions: Do existing relayer implementations default to CACHE_NOTHING?
Can an attacker construct a proof that FORCES caching via specific hop conditions?
Is the caching feature intended to be used only via direct SignalService calls?
validation_requirements: Submit processMessage with a proof having CACHE_BOTH and verify it reverts with B_NOT_RECEIVED
Verify same proof succeeds with CACHE_NOTHING
Check if any non-last-hop condition forces _syncChainData even with CACHE_NOTHING

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; DoS impact on message processing
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent until relayers learn to use CACHE_NOTHING

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users whose messages are processed with caching proofs
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Message stuck until re-proven with CACHE_NOTHING
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All messages attempted with non-zero CacheOption

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test CacheOption values through Bridge's staticcall path
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm SSTORE in staticcall causes revert

threats.title: Permanent Message DoS via Reverting refundTo Address in processMessage
threats.hypothesis_description: In processMessage, ETH refunds are pushed to refundTo via LibAddress.sendEther, which reverts if the transfer fails. If refundTo is a contract that cannot receive ETH (or reverts on receive), the entire processMessage transaction reverts. The message remains permanently stuck in NEW status since every processing attempt fails at the refund step. The message cannot be recalled on the source chain either (status is not FAILED).
threats.investigation_rationale: LibAddress.sendEther checks success and reverts on failure. Since processMessage pushes both fee and refundAmount via sendEther, a reverting refundTo permanently blocks message processing. Even with refundAmount=0, sendEther(address, 0) still calls the target which can revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: refundTo.sendEther(refundAmount) can revert if refundTo is a contract that rejects ETH, permanently blocking processMessage

preconditions: refundTo set to a contract address that reverts on ETH receipt (e.g., contract without receive/fallback)
Message is in NEW status with valid proof on destination chain
msg.sender != refundTo (separate refund path where refundTo.sendEther(refundAmount) is called)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: refundTo.sendEther(refundAmount) uses ExcessivelySafeCall which returns false → sendEther reverts
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User (or proxy wallet) sets refundTo to reverting contract. Message value + fee become permanently locked in Bridge.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Self-griefing primarily, but funds locked in Bridge reduce protocol solvency for other messages

economic_questions: What fraction of bridge messages have custom refundTo addresses?
Can the destOwner process (using gasleft()) to bypass the refund issue?
Does the same refundTo DoS affect recallMessage flow?
validation_requirements: Deploy reverting contract, send message with it as refundTo, verify processMessage permanently reverts
Verify the message can never transition from NEW status
Check if destOwner path (gasleft()) has same issue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for sendMessage + deploy reverting contract

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Individual message locking, cumulative effect on Bridge balance

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Message sender (self-inflicted) and Bridge liquidity pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of message value + fee

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test sendEther behavior with various non-receiving contracts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm processMessage permanently fails and message cannot transition

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch Drains ERC20Vault on Destination Chain
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Vault._handleMessage on the source chain correctly accounts for fee-on-transfer tokens via balance delta measurement. However, _transferTokens on the destination chain calls `IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount)` with the full recorded amount. If the canonical token on the destination chain has transfer fees, the recipient receives less than _amount but the vault's accounting considers the full amount transferred. Over multiple bridge cycles, the vault's canonical token balance becomes insufficient to back all bridged tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Source-side accounting uses balance delta (correct), but destination-side assumes full transfer (incorrect for fee-on-transfer tokens). This asymmetry creates a slow drain of canonical token reserves from the destination vault.
deployment_assumptions: Canonical ERC20 token on destination chain has transfer fees (fee-on-transfer behavior)
threats.config_evidence: ERC20Vault.sol:_handleMessage lines 300-314 measures balance delta; _transferTokens line 266 does safeTransfer(_to, _amount) without delta check. No deployment evidence of specific fee-on-transfer tokens being used.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount) transfers full amount without measuring actual balance change on destination

preconditions: Canonical token on destination chain is a fee-on-transfer token
Token is bridged across chains where destination holds canonical tokens
Multiple bridge operations occur accumulating the deficit

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeTransfer(_to, _amount) where actual transfer = _amount - fee; vault balance decreases by _amount but recipient gets less
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker repeatedly bridges fee-on-transfer token back and forth, each cycle draining vault by the fee amount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each cycle drains fee_percentage * amount from vault. After N cycles, vault becomes insolvent.

economic_questions: Are any fee-on-transfer tokens commonly bridged through Taiko?
What is the cumulative deficit per cycle?
At what point does the vault become insolvent?
validation_requirements: Deploy fee-on-transfer token, bridge back and forth, measure vault balance deficit
Calculate number of cycles to insolvency for various fee percentages
Verify source-side delta measurement doesn't compensate for destination-side deficit

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Token amount for bridging (recovered partially each cycle)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for two bridge operations per cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to token transfer fee (e.g., 1% fee = 1% drain per cycle)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time with fee-on-transfer token configured
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Vault insolvency affects all holders of that bridged token

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All bridged token holders; last redeemers cannot withdraw
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to their share of bridged tokens
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire canonical token balance in vault

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Survey tokens bridged through Taiko vaults for fee-on-transfer behavior
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Measure actual transfer amounts vs recorded amounts

threats.title: Suspend/Unsuspend Bypasses Proof Verification Enabling Fabricated Message Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: A compromised bridge_watchdog can call suspendMessages(hashes, true) then suspendMessages(hashes, false) for any arbitrary msgHash. The unsuspend sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = block.timestamp. In processMessage, receivedAt != 0 causes isMessageProven = true, completely SKIPPING Merkle proof verification via _proveSignalReceived. Since messageStatus defaults to NEW (0) for all hashes, ANY crafted message matching the suspended-then-unsuspended hash can be executed without cross-chain proof. Unlike recallMessage (which has isSignalSent check), processMessage has NO secondary verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is the most critical code-level vulnerability identified. processMessage's only defense against fabricated messages is _proveSignalReceived, and the suspend/unsuspend mechanism creates a path to bypass it entirely. The asymmetry with recallMessage (which has isSignalSent check) confirms this is unintentional.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: bridge_watchdog is not registered in deployment scripts, meaning only owner can call suspendMessages. But AddressManager can be updated post-deployment to add a bridge_watchdog address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: suspendMessages sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = uint64(block.timestamp) on unsuspend; processMessage lines 188-191 check receivedAt != 0 to skip proof

preconditions: Attacker has compromised bridge_watchdog key (or owner key)
Bridge holds ETH or vaults hold tokens to be drained
Attacker can compute message hash for crafted message

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = uint64(block.timestamp) WITHOUT any proof verification
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Craft message M, compute H=hashMessage(M), suspend+unsuspend H, wait invocationDelay, call processMessage(M, emptyProof)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Drain entire Bridge ETH balance + trigger arbitrary vault operations (mint bridged tokens, release canonical tokens)

economic_questions: What is the total ETH held by Bridge across all chains?
What is the total canonical token value held by vaults?
Is bridge_watchdog role currently assigned to any address?
validation_requirements: Verify suspendMessages(hash, true) + suspendMessages(hash, false) creates valid proofReceipt
Verify processMessage skips _proveSignalReceived when isMessageProven=true
Confirm no secondary verification exists in processMessage (unlike recallMessage's isSignalSent)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for 3 transactions: suspend, unsuspend, processMessage
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire Bridge ETH balance + all vault token balances

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All Bridge users and bridged token holders

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if bridge_watchdog is registered in production AddressManager
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Build PoC: suspend→unsuspend→processMessage with fabricated message

threats.title: Banned Vault Address Causes Permanent Token Loss for In-Flight Messages
threats.hypothesis_description: When addressBanned[_message.to] is true, processMessage marks the message as DONE with value refunded, but the message invocation (onMessageInvocation) is never executed. For vault bridge transfers, tokens were already locked/burned on the source chain. Since status is DONE (not FAILED), recallMessage on the source chain fails (requires NEW status). The encoded token data is permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ban mechanism creates an unrecoverable state for in-flight token messages. DONE status prevents recall, while the invocation never executes to release/mint tokens on destination. This violates token conservation invariants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addressBanned[_message.to] → refundAmount = _message.value, status = DONE. Token data in _message.data never processed.

preconditions: bridge_watchdog or owner bans the destination vault address
Token bridge messages targeting that vault are in-flight (status NEW)
Someone processes these messages after the ban is effective

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Status set to DONE → recallMessage requires status == NEW → recall impossible
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Ban vault address → process in-flight token messages → tokens permanently locked on source, no release on dest
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker (compromised watchdog) could ban vault, lock victim tokens, then unban for future exploitation

economic_questions: What is the typical volume of in-flight vault messages at any time?
Could the ban be temporary enough to affect specific high-value messages?
Is there any admin override to recover tokens from DONE messages?
validation_requirements: Ban vault address, process token message, verify DONE status prevents recall
Verify tokens are permanently locked on source chain
Check if unban allows recovery (it doesn't - status is already DONE)

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of all in-flight token messages to banned vault
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% loss for victims; no direct profit for attacker (griefing)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent token loss from single ban action

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When high-value token transfers are in-flight
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Potentially significant for affected token holders

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with in-flight token bridge messages to banned vault
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of bridged tokens for that message
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all in-flight token transfer values

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Identify typical in-flight message volume and value
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify DONE status on banned target prevents all recovery paths

threats.title: Unchecked Overflow in invocationDelay Addition Could Bypass Security Delay
threats.hypothesis_description: In processMessage, the code uses `unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; }` when the caller is not the preferredExecutor. Since getInvocationDelays is a virtual function that can be overridden in derived contracts, a custom implementation returning large uint256 values could cause this unchecked addition to wrap around to a small number, effectively eliminating the security delay on L1.
threats.investigation_rationale: While current hardcoded values (3600 + 384) are safe, the virtual function pattern combined with unchecked arithmetic creates a latent vulnerability. Any derived Bridge contract overriding getInvocationDelays with values approaching uint256.max would break the time-based security mechanism.
threats.config_evidence: Bridge.sol getInvocationDelays is declared virtual at line 267. Current values are hardcoded and safe.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; } where both values come from virtual function

preconditions: Derived Bridge contract overrides getInvocationDelays with large values
Sum of invocationDelay + invocationExtraDelay overflows uint256
Non-preferred executor calls processMessage after overflow reduces effective delay

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay } where both are uint256 from virtual function
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If overridden values sum to > uint256.max, wrapped delay is near 0, bypassing security window
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Bypass invocation delay → process malicious messages before watchdog can suspend

economic_questions: Are there any deployed derived Bridge contracts?
Could a malicious upgrade introduce overflow-prone delay values?
What is the economic impact of bypassing the 1-hour security delay on L1?
validation_requirements: Deploy derived Bridge with near-max delay values, verify overflow produces small effective delay
Assess likelihood of upgrade introducing this issue

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Eliminates watchdog reaction window on L1
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Enables immediate execution of time-sensitive exploits

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L1 Bridge users relying on invocation delay for security

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current hardcoded values are safe; check for derived contracts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify uint256 overflow behavior in unchecked block

threats.title: SignalService Cannot Be Paused During Active Exploit Due to _authorizePause Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService._authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, making the SignalService permanently unpausable. If a vulnerability is discovered in Merkle proof verification (LibTrieProof/MerkleTrie) or in the multi-hop proof logic, there is no way to halt proof processing. All bridge operations that depend on proof verification continue to be exploitable even during an active attack.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike Bridge and Vaults which can be paused via bridge_pauser, SignalService has no emergency stop. Since all cross-chain proof verification flows through SignalService.proveSignalReceived (called by Bridge via staticcall), an unpausable SignalService means the entire proof layer cannot be shut down.
threats.config_evidence: SignalService.sol line 194: function _authorizePause(address) internal pure override { revert SS_UNSUPPORTED(); }

proof_of_signal.pattern: _authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, preventing any pause

preconditions: A vulnerability exists in LibTrieProof or MerkleTrie proof verification
Active exploitation is occurring through forged proofs
Bridge is paused but SignalService continues processing proofs via direct calls

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: During proof verification exploit, SignalService continues accepting and caching potentially malicious proofs/chain data
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extended exploitation window during incident response; no emergency brake for proof layer

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk if proof verification is compromised?
Does pausing Bridge effectively prevent exploitation even with SignalService running?
Can syncChainData by authorized addresses poison state even while Bridge is paused?
validation_requirements: Verify SignalService.pause() reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED
Assess whether pausing Bridge alone is sufficient defense
Check if direct calls to proveSignalReceived (non-staticcall) can still cache malicious data during Bridge pause

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on the underlying proof verification vulnerability
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Extended exploitation window compared to pausable service

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Extends damage window for any proof-based exploit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users if proof verification is compromised
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potentially total loss of bridged assets
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol TVL across all chains

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify no alternative pause mechanism exists for SignalService
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm Bridge pause is sufficient to prevent exploitation via proof caching

threats.title: ERC1155Vault Uses Unchecked Block Around External Calls in _handleMessage
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC1155Vault._handleMessage wraps the entire token handling logic in an `unchecked` block, including external calls to BridgedERC1155.burn() and IERC1155.safeTransferFrom(). While the loop counter overflow is protected by MAX_TOKEN_PER_TXN=10, the unchecked block also applies to any arithmetic within the external call context setup. If future modifications add arithmetic within this unchecked scope, overflow/underflow protection is silently disabled.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked block is overly broad - it should only wrap the loop increment, not the entire function body including external calls. This is a code quality issue that creates latent risk for future modifications. The same pattern exists in ERC721Vault._handleMessage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { ... entire if-else with external calls ... } - overly broad unchecked scope

preconditions: Current code has no arithmetic overflow within the unchecked scope beyond loop counter
Future modification adds arithmetic within the unchecked block
Overflow occurs silently without revert

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unchecked block wraps external calls and all intermediate logic, not just loop counter
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Currently safe, but any added arithmetic within scope loses overflow protection
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Latent risk; no immediate exploit with current code

economic_questions: Is there any arithmetic within the unchecked scope besides the loop counter?
Could a malicious ERC1155 token trigger arithmetic in this scope?
Does the same pattern in ERC721Vault have the same risk?
validation_requirements: Audit all operations within the unchecked block for implicit arithmetic
Verify external calls cannot trigger overflow-relevant arithmetic in caller's unchecked context

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: None currently; risk for future vulnerabilities

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After code modification within unchecked scope
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Depends on nature of future modification

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all operations within unchecked blocks in both vaults
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm no current arithmetic besides loop counter
